project: The Concurrent 3280 — Jersey's Last Great Pre-RISC Mini
total_runtime: ~8:09 narration (v1 audio review render); storyboard target ~12 min with B-roll pacing
framing_note: STANDALONE documentary (not Episode 2 of Jersey Stack — decision May 6 2026). About the machine and its architect Ken Yeager. Nick stays behind the camera. The phone-list reveal in Scene 17 is the only personal anchor and runs SILENT.

scenes.title: The thesis lands first
scenes.narration: These two machines sit thirty feet apart in a museum in Wall, New Jersey. One is the last great minicomputer of an era that's about to end. The other is the chip that ended it. The same engineer designed both.
scenes.visual: Slow push through InfoAge floor. Static shot of the Concurrent 3280 cabinet, hold 4s. Cut to the SGI Onyx 10000 across the room, hold 4s. Cut back to a wide that frames both, with a measuring distance overlay reading '~30 ft.'
scenes.image_prompt: Live action — InfoAge B-roll required. New shoot: 3280 cabinet (wide + medium + push-in), SGI Onyx 10000 wide, then frame-both wide with overlay added in post. No AI generation — practical museum footage only.
scenes.text_overlay: ~30 ft. (overlay on the wide framing both machines)
scenes.music: No music for first 8 seconds. Low drone fades in under 'thirty feet apart' line.

scenes.title: Title card (silent — STANDALONE)
scenes.visual: Hard cut to black. Title animates in. Heavy serif main title 'THE CONCURRENT 3280'. Subtitle thinner sans: 'Jersey's Last Great Pre-RISC Mini.' STANDALONE — no Episode 2 mark, no Jersey Stack series brand.
scenes.image_prompt: Title card built in FFmpeg drawtext / motion template. Black with subtle noise texture. Main title heavy serif, subtitle lighter sans. No series mark. [Motion graphic — not an AI image]
scenes.text_overlay: THE CONCURRENT 3280 / Jersey's Last Great Pre-RISC Mini
scenes.music: Single sustained note, drops into low bed.

scenes.title: Edison NJ, 1966 — Interdata founded
scenes.narration: Edison, New Jersey. 1966. A handful of engineers walk out of Electronic Associates and start a company called Interdata. Their bet: a 32-bit minicomputer at a time when the rest of the industry is still arguing about 16. Six years later they ship the 7/32 — one of the first 32-bit minis on the market, designed and built in New Jersey.
scenes.visual: Period photo wash — Edison NJ aerial / industrial 1960s. Cut to Interdata 7/32 marketing photo. Push in slowly on the front panel.
scenes.image_prompt: Period archival imagery: 1960s Edison NJ aerial / industrial. Interdata 7/32 marketing photos from Wikipedia / Computer History Museum / Bitsavers. Stock 1960s NJ industrial B-roll.

scenes.title: The 8/32 and the Perkin-Elmer years
scenes.narration: 1975 — Interdata ships the 8/32, faster, refined for industrial and real-time work. By 1973 Perkin-Elmer had already bought the company for $73 million and rebranded it Data Systems. The Interdata machines went where DEC and IBM didn't want to go: refineries, weapons systems, factory floors. Quiet, reliable, mostly invisible.
scenes.visual: Interdata 8/32 photo. Slow morph from 'Interdata' to 'Perkin-Elmer Data Systems' branding on the same chassis. Brief inserts: oil rig, missile-defense console, telemetry rack.
scenes.image_prompt: Interdata 8/32 photos from Bitsavers. Perkin-Elmer logo evolution. Industrial stock B-roll: oil refinery, missile defense rack, factory telemetry.

scenes.title: November 1985 — the Concurrent spinoff
scenes.narration: November 1985. Perkin-Elmer spins the data systems group off as a standalone company. They call it Concurrent Computer Corporation. Headquarters: Tinton Falls, New Jersey. And inside that building, a small team is already deep into the design of the machine that will become the company's flagship — the Concurrent 3280.
scenes.visual: Headline-style graphic: 'November 1985: Perkin-Elmer spins off Data Systems → Concurrent Computer Corporation.' Tinton Falls NJ exterior. Animated map pin on Tinton Falls.
scenes.image_prompt: 1985 NJ business news clippings if findable. Tinton Falls map graphic. Concurrent Computer logo. Period exterior of the Tinton Falls campus.

scenes.title: ⭐ Inside the lab, Christmas 1985 (centerpiece)
scenes.narration: These photographs were taken inside the Concurrent engineering lab in Tinton Falls in December 1985. They are, as far as anyone has been able to tell, the only known photographs of the 3280 in development. The machine in the cabinet would not be announced for another two years.
scenes.visual: ⭐ THE EMOTIONAL CENTERPIECE. Nick's Christmas 1985 lab photos. Slow Ken-Burns across the actual lab. The 3280 cabinet under fluorescent light. Engineer's desk strewn with logic analyzer printouts. Whiteboard. Pace these slowly — these are the rarest assets in the video.
scenes.image_prompt: Nick's 1985 lab photos (creator-owned primary source). Highest-resolution scans available. NO AI generation — these are the rarest assets in the entire production.
scenes.music: Drops to almost nothing. Reverent but not sentimental. Let silence breathe.

scenes.title: The architect at work (observational)
scenes.narration: The architect of the 3280 was Ken Yeager. MIT, class of '72. He'd come to Concurrent — then still Perkin-Elmer — in 1979. He rode his bicycle to work every day. His pants cuffs stayed rolled up at the ankle, even at his desk. He wore the same kind of checkered, bowling-style shirt every week. He hummed, almost constantly, while he worked. He stayed in his office. Engineers had audiences with him — they'd go in, come out with a scrap of paper covered in Yeager's handwriting, and they'd execute on it. The 3280 was designed on those scraps.
scenes.visual: Yeager portrait (sourced from Mike Martone's Facebook archive). Cutaway shots evoking observations: bicycle leaning on a wall, ankle-cuffed jeans, checkered bowling shirt on a coatrack, torn scrap of notebook paper with hand-drawn logic sketch. NO actors — objects and stills only.
scenes.image_prompt: Yeager portrait: pulled from Mike Martone's Facebook posts in the ex-Concurrent group (Nick has identified the photo). Stylized stills: bicycle, checkered shirt, scrap of paper with hand-sketched ALU stage. Possibly one AI illustration of a 'Yeager scrap.'
scenes.text_overlay: KEN YEAGER · MIT '72 · ARCHITECT (lower-third on portrait)

scenes.title: The four-board pipeline (STILLS, not animation)
scenes.narration: The 3280's CPU was four boards. Each board was one stage of a scalar pipeline: fetch, decode, ALU, write-back. Four hundred nanoseconds per stage. One instruction every clock cycle. The whole thing was built from Schottky TTL — discrete logic chips, soldered to printed-circuit boards, hand-routed. By 1988 this was anachronistic. The rest of the industry was moving to single-chip CPUs. But for real-time work — refineries, defense, simulators — the 3280's discrete-logic pipeline was deterministic, predictable, and proven. The engineering team was small — four boards, one architect, a senior engineer consulting across all of them, an engineer on loan from another group, and the technician who actually built the prototype on the bench. The architect handed out the scraps. The team turned the scraps into silicon and copper.
scenes.visual: STILL diagram (no animation): FETCH → DECODE → ALU → WRITE-BACK with arrows. Then dissolve to photo macros of Schottky TTL boards (reshoot from the InfoAge unit). Each board card flies in with its label.
scenes.image_prompt: Static infographic: four-stage pipeline FETCH → DECODE → ALU → WRITE-BACK. Then macro photography of InfoAge 3280 boards. Possible diagram callout for the S-Bus (F-PATH/T-PATH).
scenes.text_overlay: FETCH → DECODE → ALU → WRITE-BACK (across pipeline still)
scenes.transition: Hard cut to Tech Monitor headline

scenes.title: January 26, 1988 — the 3280SP
scenes.narration: January 26, 1988. Concurrent announces the 3280SP — single processor, six and a half MIPS, up to 32 megabytes of RAM, a one-gigabyte disk. List price in the UK: £200,000. It runs Concurrent's own real-time operating system, OS/32, alongside a Unix port called XELOS.
scenes.visual: Tech Monitor headline: 'Concurrent Introduces 6 MIPS Single Processor 3280.' Date stamp: January 26, 1988. Cut to a 3280SP product photo. Specs sheet animates in: 6.4 MIPS, 32 MB RAM, 1 GB disk, OS/32 + Unix V.
scenes.image_prompt: Tech Monitor archive scrape (Jan 26, 1988 issue). 3280SP product imagery if available; otherwise stylized 1988-era press-clipping graphic.

scenes.title: November 29, 1988 — the 3280E MPS
scenes.narration: Eleven months later, November 29, 1988 — Concurrent announces the 3280E MPS. Up to 12 processors in a single cabinet. 76.8 aggregate MIPS. 256 megabytes of memory. $1.8 million, fully loaded. The processors talked to each other over a synchronous bus called the S-Bus, on a patent filed by Ken Yeager in 1986. This was the most powerful machine the Interdata lineage ever produced. And it would be the last.
scenes.visual: Second Tech Monitor headline: 'New High-Speed ECL Bus for Concurrent's 3280 MPS Supermini.' Spec card animates: up to 12 processors, 76.8 MIPS aggregate, 256 MB RAM, $1.8M. Cut to a 3280E MPS rack diagram. Animated bus diagram showing CPUs over the S-Bus.
scenes.image_prompt: Tech Monitor archive (Nov 29, 1988). S-Bus patent diagram from DE3722458A1 figure (Google Patents). 3280E MPS imagery if recoverable.
scenes.music: Crescendo. Last sentence is the pivot — drop on 'And it would be the last.'

scenes.title: The phone list foreshadow + Mike Martone
scenes.narration: By the end of 1988, the 3280 was Concurrent's flagship. Inside that lab in Tinton Falls there were maybe twenty people who knew how it actually worked. Most of them are not on the public internet. Most of them have no Wikipedia page, no LinkedIn, no obituary. The technician who built the prototype, a man named Mike Martone, is the one keeping the machine's memory alive — he posts about it, almost forty years later, in a small Facebook group of ex-Concurrent engineers.
scenes.visual: Quick cut to a corner of the lab phone list. Hold 3 seconds, just long enough to be noticed. Don't dwell. Then cut to a wider shot of the engineering team area (Nick's 1985 photos again — different angle from Scene 6).
scenes.image_prompt: Lab phone list (partial — full reveal saved for Scene 17). Pulled from Mike Martone's Facebook archive. Facebook group screenshot. Mike Martone post excerpt.
scenes.music: Quiet. Setting up the 'history disappears' beat.

scenes.title: Yeager leaves Concurrent
scenes.narration: Around 1990, Ken Yeager leaves Concurrent. He goes west — Silicon Graphics, MIPS Computer Systems, in Sunnyvale, California. He arrives in time for the next great processor war. The industry has finally figured out how to put a 32-bit CPU on a single piece of silicon. The minicomputer is about to become a museum piece. And Yeager — the man who built the 3280 from four boards of Schottky TTL — is going to help design what replaces it.
scenes.visual: Map graphic: NJ → CA. SGI logo emerges. Then a 1990s-era SGI/MIPS office photo, or an MIPS R-series chip die photo.
scenes.image_prompt: SGI/MIPS imagery from the early 1990s. Possible Yeager-at-SGI photo if surfaced via outreach. Period MIPS R-series die shots.

scenes.title: The R10000 payoff
scenes.narration: In 1996, Yeager publishes a paper in IEEE Micro. The title: 'The MIPS R10000 Superscalar Microprocessor.' Four-instruction issue. Out-of-order execution. Speculative execution. A textbook RISC processor — the kind they teach computer architecture grad students with. One chip, one piece of silicon, more performance than the 3280 had with 12 CPUs and a $1.8M price tag. Yeager went on to architect the R12000 and the R18000. He held four sole patents and ten team patents during those years. The chips he designed at MIPS shipped inside Silicon Graphics workstations — the machines that rendered Jurassic Park, Toy Story, the early web.
scenes.visual: Yeager's R10000 paper cover (IEEE Micro 1996). R10000 die shot. Hardware photo of the R10000 chip in package. Static callout: '4-issue out-of-order superscalar.' Then a wide shot of an SGI Onyx workstation. Cut to InfoAge: a real Onyx 10000 in the museum.
scenes.image_prompt: R10000 paper. Die shot from public sources. Onyx imagery. Brief Jurassic Park / Toy Story render reference (fair-use clip — keep brief).
scenes.music: Pace deliberate, not rushed. Earn the payoff.

scenes.title: August 2017 (restraint)
scenes.narration: Ken Yeager died in August 2017. He was 66. The same year, Concurrent Computer Corporation was acquired and ceased to exist as an independent company.
scenes.visual: Black background. Single line of text fades in: 'Kenneth C. Yeager, MIT '72 — August 10, 2017.' Hold. No photo. No music swell. Just text. Then fade.
scenes.image_prompt: Text card only — black background, white serif text.
scenes.music: Drop music to ZERO. Hold black. Restraint is the point.

scenes.title: The InfoAge survivor (2026)
scenes.narration: There are only a handful of Concurrent 3280s left in the world. One of them sits at the InfoAge Science Center in Wall, New Jersey — a former Cold War Army communications base, now a computer museum. The cabinet is intact. The four pipeline boards are still in their slots. The Schottky TTL chips are still soldered where Mike Martone soldered them. The machine has not been powered on in years.
scenes.visual: Present-day InfoAge B-roll. The 3280 cabinet in its current museum location. Wide → medium → boards detail. Possibly a gloved hand lifting one of the pipeline boards out — slow, careful.
scenes.image_prompt: Live action — Nick to shoot at InfoAge. Sequence: wide / medium / boards detail / hand-on-board.
scenes.transition: Slow pull-back into the museum wide

scenes.title: 30 feet apart — the placard line lands
scenes.narration: The chip that powered the SGI Onyx 10000 — also in this museum, about thirty feet from the 3280 — is the MIPS R10000. Ken Yeager designed both. The minicomputer he built in New Jersey, and the chip in California that helped replace it. They retired into the same room.
scenes.visual: Push out from the 3280 — pull back through the museum floor — land on the SGI Onyx 10000 across the room. Frame both in one wide shot. Overlay placard line in clean type: 'Designed in NJ. Built in NJ. The architect who built it shaped the chip that replaced it. And both machines now sit retired in this museum — about thirty feet apart.'
scenes.image_prompt: Wide museum shot framing both machines. Type overlay applied in post.
scenes.text_overlay: Designed in NJ. Built in NJ. The architect who built it shaped the chip that replaced it. And both machines now sit retired in this museum — about thirty feet apart.
scenes.music: Land the thesis. Bed swells subtly under placard line.

scenes.title: The phone list (silent grace note)
scenes.visual: Cut to black. Then fade up on the lab phone list — full frame, dot-matrix print. Slow vertical scroll. Stop on a name about two-thirds down: 'DeMarco, N. — Co-op.' Hold 6 seconds. **All other names blurred** (per May 6 decision). NO narration. NO music. Just the artifact.
scenes.image_prompt: Lab phone list scan from Mike Martone's Facebook archive. Reshoot at high resolution. Apply blur in post to all names except 'DeMarco, N. — Co-op.'

scenes.title: Credits / outro (STANDALONE)
scenes.narration: If you worked at Concurrent — or knew someone who did — find us in the comments.
scenes.visual: Standalone credits template (no Jersey Stack series mark, no Ep 2 callouts). Sources: Tech Monitor 1988, IEEE Micro 1996, Bitsavers, ACM Digital Library, the Concurrent alumni Facebook group, and the InfoAge Science Center. Special thanks to Mike Martone.
